text:
This paper describes a state-of-the-art supervised statistical text classification system for classifying short informal text samples in the domain of sentiment analysis. The present system performs two kinds of classification tasks: message level sentiment classification (i.e., for determining the polarity of an entire message like a tweet or an SMS) and term level sentiment classification (i.e., for determining the polarity of individual terms or phrases appearing in a given context.

We focused on a diverse set of features which include surface form, semantic and specialized sentiment features. Our work uniquely contributes to the creation of high-coverage, fine grained tweet-level sentiment lexicons which can be automatically derived from a large dataset (millions of tweets with emoticons and sentiment expressing hashtags). To deal with the well known negation phenomenon we have specialized negation-sensitive lexicons to capture word sentiments in the affirmative and negative contexts differently.

When evaluated on the datasets from the SemEval-2013 competition, our system achieved the best performance, i.e., first place, on the message-level and term-level tasks for Twitter, and showed surprisingly robust cross-domain performance on SMS and movie reviews. The ablation experiments showed that the automatically generated lexicons for negation and their application for strengthening negation in training had the largest impact, with the system performing up to 6.5 absolute percentage points worse. A detailed analysis of the empirical estimates of negated terms derived from a corpus confirms that they are greatly preferable to the traditional polarity-reversal heuristics.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
